Moses has every reason to doubt. He stammers, he's uncertain, he's been away from Egypt for forty years—and now God is asking him to stand before Pharaoh and demand the freedom of an entire nation. So when Moses asks what will happen if the people don't believe him, his question isn't abstract theology; it's the voice of a man staring at an impossible task. God doesn't dismiss his fear. Instead, He gives him proof. The staff becomes a snake. His hand becomes leprous then whole again. These aren't magic tricks meant to amuse; they're signs that God Himself has appeared, that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—the God who made covenants centuries ago—is still alive, still active, still keeping His promises. God is answering Moses' deepest fear with evidence: you won't be alone in this, and people will know it.

But Moses keeps objecting. He's not eloquent. His speech is slow. And with each objection, he's really saying the same thing: I'm not capable. What's striking is that God's patience wears thin. The Lord's anger burns against Moses. This is the moment where God stops reassuring and starts commanding. And in His anger, there's a question that cuts to the heart of Moses' complaint: "Who gave man his mouth?" God is reminding Moses that eloquence itself comes from the Creator. The ability to speak, to think, to communicate—these are God's gifts, not human achievements. By refusing to trust what God can do through his weakness, Moses isn't just expressing doubt about the mission; he's doubting God's sovereignty over the very faculties He created. So God arranges for Aaron to be Moses' voice, but He does it in a way that makes clear: this concession doesn't change what's true. You will speak. I will be with you. The staff in your hand—which has already done the impossible—will do it again.

What follows is a strange and violent turn. After all these reassurances, God meets Moses on the road and threatens to kill him. Zipporah, Moses' wife, circumcises their son and touches the blood to Moses' feet, and only then does God relent. The text doesn't explain why God's anger burns here, but the answer lies in what's been left undone. Moses has accepted his calling, received his signs, and begun his journey—yet his own son remains uncircumcised, outside the covenant of Abraham. Moses is about to demand that Pharaoh release God's firstborn son, Israel, but he has neglected the sign of the covenant on his own family. This isn't a small oversight. This is hypocrisy about to unfold in the presence of the most powerful man on earth. God's violence here is the mercy of stopping a man from walking into catastrophe while bearing witness to a covenant he hasn't even kept in his own household. Only after this brutal correction does Aaron meet him, does Moses rehearse everything with the elders, and do the people finally believe.

What Moses encounters in this chapter is the gap between the God he thinks he needs and the God who actually is. He needs eloquence; God offers His own words. He needs confidence; God offers signs and His presence. He needs time to prepare; God demands obedience now. And most painfully, he needs to understand that accepting God's call isn't a matter of being adequate—it's a matter of being aligned with God's purposes, even in the most intimate corners of your life. The people do believe. They do worship. But it only happens after Moses stops negotiating with God and starts obeying Him, even when obedience comes through his wife's desperate act and God's terrible correction. This is the cost of being God's messenger: not safety or eloquence or certainty, but alignment with His will at whatever price it demands.
